summary: Spolsky introduces his 'Law of Leaky Abstractions,' arguing that all non-trivial abstractions inevitably fail to fully hide the complexity beneath them. He illustrates this with TCP/IP as a primary example — TCP builds reliability on top of unreliable IP, but network failures still leak through. He extends this to SQL query performance, C++ strings, NFS file systems, ASP.NET, and even driving in rain. The key consequence is that while abstractions let us handle greater complexity, they don't reduce the amount developers must learn, because when abstractions break, you need to understand the underlying layers. Paradoxically, as programming tools become more powerful and abstract, becoming a proficient programmer becomes harder, not easier.
key_points: All non-trivial abstractions are leaky — they fail to completely hide underlying complexity, and the failures manifest as bugs, performance problems, or mysterious behavior.
TCP/IP perfectly illustrates leaky abstraction: TCP builds reliability atop unreliable IP, but network failures, congestion, and severed cables still leak through.
Abstractions save us time working but don't save us time learning — developers must still understand the layers beneath their tools to debug inevitable leaks.
As programming tools become higher-level with better abstractions, becoming a proficient programmer paradoxically becomes harder because there are more layers to understand when things go wrong.
Code generation tools and 'wizzy' productivity shortcuts are especially prone to this problem — when they break, users who skipped learning the fundamentals are completely stuck.
money_quotes: All non-trivial abstractions, to some degree, are leaky.

TCP attempts to provide a complete abstraction of an underlying unreliable network, but sometimes, the network leaks through the abstraction and you feel the things that the abstraction can't quite protect you from.
The abstractions save us time working, but they don't save us time learning.
And all this means that paradoxically, even as we have higher and higher level programming tools with better and better abstractions, becoming a proficient programmer is getting harder and harder.
When you need to hire a programmer to do mostly VB programming, it's not good enough to hire a VB programmer, because they will get completely stuck in tar every time the VB abstraction leaks.
Learn how to do it manually first, then use the wizzy tool to save time.
The Law of Leaky Abstractions is dragging us down.
One day they'll write the code "foo" + "bar", and truly bizarre things will happen, and then I'll have to stop and teach them all about char*'s anyway.
key_insight: Abstractions save working time but not learning time — every layer of abstraction a programmer uses is another layer they must eventually understand when it inevitably leaks.
